title: Electron-Ion Path Integral Monte Carlo with Hard Core
abstract: We performed numerical (restricted) path integral Monte Carlo experiments on metallic Hydrogen from first principles. We study a quantum two component plasma where one component is made of pointwise particles of negative unitary charge and the other is made of charged hard spheres of positive unitary charge. We study both the additive mixture and a nonadditive mixture where we only keep a hard core between unlike species. We specialize to the case of the electron-proton plasma with a 1:1 ratios between the molar fraction of the two species. We measured thermodynamic and structural properties of the plasma. From an analysis of the structure we see a transition from a metallic Hydrogen phase, to a molecular Hydrogen phase as the temperature is lowered. As expected at high density the correlations are diminished.
summary: This work uses Path Integral Monte Carlo simulations to study electron-proton plasma, aiming to predict the formation of molecular hydrogen. By analyzing structural correlations, the authors demonstrate that quantum statistical effects predict a phase transition from a metallic to a molecular hydrogen state upon cooling. This research is significant for understanding quantum matter under extreme conditions.
main_problem: To investigate the quantum statistical prediction of molecular hydrogen formation within a plasma composed of electrons and protons from first principles.
main_result: The simulations predict a phase transition sequence from metallic to molecular hydrogen as temperature decreases, and the structure analysis confirms the formation of $	ext{H}_2$ molecules.
method: Path Integral Monte Carlo (PIMC) simulations are used to calculate thermodynamic and structural properties of the plasma.
model_or_system: The system is a quantum two-component plasma (electron-proton plasma) modeled by a Hamiltonian including Coulomb interactions and a hard core for stabilization.
key_observables: Thermodynamic properties, structural properties, radial distribution functions ($g_{ij}(r)$), and superfluid fractions.
important_parameters: Reduced temperature ($T$), reduced number density ($n$), and the comparison between Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac statistics.
main_assumptions: The use of Restricted PIMC (RPIMC) for fermions due to the sign problem, and the necessity of an artificial hard core ($\sigma$) in the primitive approximation.
figures_summary: Figures show radial distribution functions ($g_{ij}(r)$) demonstrating the development of distinct peaks corresponding to $	ext{H}_2$ and $	ext{H}_3$ molecular clusters at low temperatures.
paper_structure: The paper analyzes the system by comparing additive and nonadditive mixture models, studying the evolution of structural correlations (RDFs) across different temperatures and quantum statistics.
why_interesting: The study uses advanced quantum statistical methods (PIMC) to model complex, strongly interacting quantum matter, providing insights into quantum phase transitions and molecular binding in extreme environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Snapshots of the simulation of a binary mixture with N = 20, x1 = 1/2, µ1 = 1, µ2 = 1000, σ = 0.4. The mixture is in a thermodynamic state with a reduced temperature T = T ′/T = 0.1 with T ′ measured in degrees Kelvin and a reduced number density n = n′L 3 = 0.1 with n′ measured in cm−3. In the path integral we chose M = 20 and used either Bose-Einstein (left panel, case A in Table I) or Fermi-Dirac (right panel, case B in Table I) statistics among like particles. Red paths are for the heavy slow species and black paths are for the light fast species.

FIG. 2. Snapshots of the simulation of a binary mixture with N = 20, x1 = 1/2, µ1 = 1, µ2 = 1836.15, σ = 1. The mixture is in a thermodynamic state with a reduced temperature T = T ′/T = 0.005 with T ′ measured in degrees Kelvin and a reduced number density n = n′L 3 = 0.16 with n′ measured in cm−3. In the path integral we chose M = 20 and used either Bose- Einstein (left panel, case G in Table I) or Fermi-Dirac (right panel, case H in Table I) statistics among like particles. Red paths are for the heavy slow protons and black paths are for the light fast electrons.

FIG. 3. The partial, gij, and total, gtot, radial distribution functions for a binary mixture with N = 20, x1 = 1/2, µ1 = 1, µ2 = 1000, σ = 0.4. In the panel for the unlike radial distribution function we also show the ground state probability distribution of the Hydrogen atom for comparison. The mixture is in a thermodynamic state with a reduced temperature T = T ′/T = 0.1 with T ′ measured in degrees Kelvin and a reduced number density n = n′L 3 = 0.1 with n′ measured in cm−3. In the path integral we chose M = 20 and used either Bose-Einstein (case A in Table I) or Fermi-Dirac (case B in Table I) statistics among like particles. In the ﬁgures r = r′/L where r′ is measured in centimeters.
